target.title: fix(telegram): prevent silent misparse of multi-colon delivery targets
target.body:
## Summary

- `parseTelegramTarget` with a greedy `(.+):(\\d+)$` regex silently misparses multi-colon targets like `chatId:topic:42:99` by swallowing `:topic:42` into `chatId`, returning `{ chatId: "chatId:topic:42", messageThreadId: 99 }`
- This corrupts session keys, chat type resolution, and downstream Telegram API calls ("chat not found")
- Switch to `([^:]+):(\\d+)$` so the colon-format parser never accepts chatId containing colons. Multi-colon inputs fall through to the full-string fallback instead of silently producing wrong results

## Real behavior proof

### Behavior or issue addressed:
Multi-colon Telegram delivery targets like `chatId:topic:42:99` were silently misparsed: the greedy `(.+)` captured `chatId:topic:42` as the chat ID, embedding the `:topic:42` residue. This caused wrong routing, wrong chat type, and downstream API failures.

### Evidence after fix:
```
Before fix:
  parseTelegramTarget("chatId:topic:42:99")
  → { chatId: "chatId:topic:42", messageThreadId: 99, chatType: "unknown" }
  // chatId wrongly contains :topic:42

After fix:
  parseTelegramTarget("chatId:topic:42:99")
  → { chatId: "chatId:topic:42:99", chatType: "unknown" }
  // Falls through to full-string fallback — no silent corruption

Regression:
  parseTelegramTarget("-1001234567890:topic:456")
  → { chatId: "-1001234567890", messageThreadId: 456, chatType: "group" }  ✅
  parseTelegramTarget("-1001234567890:99")
  → { chatId: "-1001234567890", messageThreadId: 99, chatType: "group" }  ✅
```

### Exact steps run after this patch:
1. Ran `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/targets.test.ts` — 44 tests passed
2. Ran regression PoC: multi-colon inputs now fall through to full-string fallback; `chatId:topic:N` and `chatId:N` formats unaffected

### Tests and validation
- `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/targets.test.ts` — 44 passed (4 new tests: multi-colon rejection x2 + regression x2)
- `npx oxlint` — no lint issues
- Manual PoC verification with `node --input-type=module`

### Risk checklist
- [x] **User-facing behavior change**: No. Existing valid formats unchanged. Malformed multi-colon inputs now correctly fall through instead of silently producing wrong chatId.
- [x] **Backward compatible**: Yes. Valid formats (`chatId`, `chatId:topic:N`, `chatId:N`) unchanged.
- [x] **Plugin SDK surface change**: No. Internal parser change only.
- [x] **Config/API surface change**: No.

Closes #15

target.title: fix(nextcloud-talk): bound bot preflight error reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`probeNextcloudTalkBotResponseFeature` reads the bot-admin error body with an unbounded `await response.text()` when the Nextcloud Talk bot-admin API returns a non-OK status during channel startup preflight. A Nextcloud endpoint that returns a large body with no `Content-Length` (broken reverse proxy, error page, or hostile/compromised host the operator pointed at) makes the gateway buffer the **entire** payload into memory before building the diagnostic — a memory-pressure vector on the startup-check path.

Affected surface: `extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/bot-preflight.ts` — the `!response.ok` branch of the bot-response feature probe.

## Why This Change Was Made

Successful JSON reads in the same probe are already bounded via `readProviderJsonResponse`, but the error branch still called `response.text()` directly. This routes that read through the existing shared bounded reader `readResponseTextLimited` (from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`) with an 8 KiB cap (`BOT_PREFLIGHT_ERROR_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES`), matching the bound already used in `send.ts`. The reader stops pulling and cancels the upstream stream once the diagnostic budget is full, so the surfaced failure message stays useful while the body can no longer grow without bound. The existing `.catch(() => "")` is preserved, so diagnostics degrade exactly as before on read failure.

No new abstraction — reuses the existing plugin-SDK helper. Plugin-local change, one call site.

## User Impact

Operators still get the same bot-preflight failure diagnostic (status + bounded body snippet) when the Nextcloud API errors during startup, but a misbehaving or hostile Nextcloud endpoint can no longer drive the gateway to buffer an arbitrarily large error body into memory.

## Evidence

**1. Real-behavior proof with a negative control (real HTTP, not mock-only).** A harness drives the **real exported** `probeNextcloudTalkBotResponseFeature` through the **real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` SSRF-guarded path** (`allowPrivateNetwork: true` to permit localhost) against a real `node:http` server that streams a **25 MiB** `503` bot-admin error body in chunks with **no `Content-Length`**, and measures bytes actually flushed on the wire before the socket closes. The negative control runs the pre-fix `await response.text()` against the same server. All endpoints are `127.0.0.1`; no secrets or private hosts involved.

```
$ tsx nc-proof.mts
Hostile bot-admin error body = 25.00 MiB, 503, streamed with NO Content-Length

[WITH FIX] probeNextcloudTalkBotResponseFeature
PASS: returns api_error — code=api_error status=503
PASS: error snippet bounded — message = 8248 bytes
PASS: upstream stopped early (cap load-bearing) — server flushed only 0.22 MiB before socket closed

[NEGATIVE CONTROL] old `await response.text()` on same server
PASS: old path buffers entire body — response.text() returned 25.01 MiB (server sent 25.01 MiB)

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

Without the fix the error read returns the full **25.01 MiB** body; with the fix the surfaced diagnostic is capped (8 KiB body + the fixed `... probe failed (503): ` prefix = 8248 bytes) and the server flushes only **0.22 MiB** before the client cancels the stream and the socket closes — proving the cap is load-bearing, not incidental. The residual ~0.22 MiB is in-flight TCP/socket buffering, not buffered by the reader.

**2. Committed regression test** (`extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/bot-preflight.test.ts`) streams an oversized error body through a tracked `ReadableStream`, asserts `response.text()` is never called, and asserts the stream is canceled after the bounded read.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/bot-preflight.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
```

**3. Lint on the changed file:**

```
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/bot-preflight.ts   # exit 0
```

**What was NOT tested:** I did not point the probe at a live production Nextcloud instance and did not drive the gateway to an actual OOM. The cap is proven by bytes-on-wire + early socket close against a local server reproducing the unbounded-stream condition over the real SSRF-guarded fetch path, plus the negative control showing the old path buffering the full body.

AI-assisted: implementation, tests, and the proof harness were drafted with agent assistance; all commands above were run locally.

target.title: feat(plugins): narrow gateway route loads from manifests
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: route activation metadata existed, but gateway plugin HTTP routing still only read the pinned startup route registry.
- Why it matters: route-capable plugins loaded after startup could not be narrowed from manifest hints, so the gateway had no real `activation.onRoutes` consumer.
- What changed: canonicalized route activation ids to `gateway-plugin-http`, added route-owner planning + trust filtering, and taught gateway plugin HTTP handling to try a scoped route registry before falling back.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no global runtime-registry rewrite, no route-path metadata model, and no removal of the pinned startup registry fallback.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `activation.onRoutes` only existed as metadata. plugin HTTP request handling resolved against the pinned startup route registry and never consulted manifest-owned route activation hints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no route-scoped registry seam and no coverage for “startup registry misses, scoped route load matches”.
- Contributing context (if known): route id naming had already drifted (`webhook`, `gateway-webhook`) before there was any live runtime consumer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server/plugins-http.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when the pinned startup route registry misses, the gateway should try a scoped manifest-planned route registry and handle the matching plugin route.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives at the gateway/plugin-registry seam, not inside route matching or manifest parsing alone.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Gateway plugin HTTP requests can now activate route-owning plugins from manifest `activation.onRoutes` hints before falling back to the pinned startup route registry.
- Route activation ids are canonicalized to `gateway-plugin-http`, while legacy `gateway-webhook` / `webhook` metadata still resolve.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[gateway request] -> [pinned startup route registry] -> [miss] -> [false]

After:
[gateway request] -> [pinned startup route registry] -> [miss]
                -> [manifest route-owner planning] -> [scoped route registry]
                -> [match] -> [handler]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: plugin route resolution can now load route-owner plugins from manifest metadata. mitigation: the new route-owner planner applies the same allow/deny/explicit-disable and non-bundled trust rules before loading any scoped route registry.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node/pnpm worktree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway plugin HTTP routes
- Relevant config (redacted): default local config plus synthetic test configs

### Steps

1. Start with a gateway startup registry that does not own the requested plugin route.
2. Plan route owners from manifest `activation.onRoutes` metadata.
3. Load the scoped route registry and re-run route matching.

### Expected

- Matching route-owner plugins are loaded through the scoped registry.
- Untrusted or disabled route owners stay excluded.
- Legacy route aliases still normalize to the canonical route id.

### Actual

- Verified locally with seam and parser/planner tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - route activation alias normalization (`webhook` / `gateway-webhook` -> `gateway-plugin-http`)
  - route-owner trust filtering for bundled/global/workspace plugins
  - gateway plugin HTTP fallback to scoped registry on startup-registry miss
- Edge cases checked:
  - non-empty allowlists still constrain route owners
  - explicit workspace disablement blocks route-owner loading
  - no planned owners means no scoped load
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full end-to-end gateway runtime with live channel/plugin traffic

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: generic `gateway-plugin-http` ownership is still coarse, so scoped route loads can include more than one candidate plugin.
  - Mitigation: keep the pinned startup registry fallback and rely on actual route matching to choose handlers; this PR does not claim per-path manifest precision.
- Risk: non-bundled route owners could become an unsafe auto-load path.
  - Mitigation: route-owner planning uses existing manifest-owner base policy plus explicit trust requirements for non-bundled plugins.

target.title: fix(llm): preserve structured tool result text across providers
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where agent/provider replay could receive literal placeholders such as `(see attached image)` or empty-output fallbacks instead of normal text/JSON tool results when a tool result block was structured but not typed as `text`.

This replacement PR covers the provider conversion paths from #96895 and the active OpenAI Responses transport path noted in review.

Closes #96857
Related: #96895

## Why This Change Was Made

Several replay paths only collected tool-result blocks with `type: "text"`. Structured non-media blocks could therefore be treated as empty output and collapse to an image/no-output fallback. This PR routes affected replay paths through one shared structured text extractor so text-like structured tool output reaches provider/model context as readable text.

Covered replay paths:

- OpenAI Responses shared conversion
- OpenAI-compatible chat completions conversion
- OpenAI Responses managed transport replay
- Google/Gemini conversion
- Anthropic tool-result replay
- Mistral tool-result replay

Current fallback behavior is preserved:

- empty non-image output remains `(no output)` / provider-equivalent no-output text where that fallback already exists;
- image-only output remains an image placeholder or image input part according to the provider path;
- media-only `image`, `image_url`, `input_image`, and `audio` blocks are not serialized into provider-bound text;
- structured non-media output is serialized as JSON text instead of being dropped by text-only filtering.

## Review Updates In Current Head

Current head: `81bbe428b7ef91907030d52e7326fbb6d0beb870`.

This update addresses the latest review findings by:

- rebasing the branch onto current `main` and preserving the empty non-image no-output fallback;
- wiring the structured extractor through Anthropic and Mistral replay instead of leaving them as text-only siblings;
- keeping explicit `text` tool-result blocks text-first, so structured serialization is only used as a no-text fallback;
- capping structured fallback replay text at 8,000 UTF-16 characters before it can cross the external provider boundary while preserving existing explicit-text replay behavior;
- reusing OpenClaw's shared structured secret redaction contract via `redactSecrets()` before provider replay;
- skipping media-only block types: `image`, `image_url`, `input_image`, and `audio`;
- omitting MIME-tagged binary `data` before provider/model replay, including `mime_type`, `mimeType`, `media_type`, `mediaType`, `content_type`, and `contentType` aliases;
- redacting actual inline data URIs while preserving ordinary prose such as `metadata:ready` and `data: is ordinary prose`;
- omitting opaque/binary fields such as `encrypted_content`, `encrypted_stdout`, `blob`, `buffer`, and `bytes`;
- adding focused helper/provider regression coverage.

## User Impact

Agents can inspect structured text-like tool output in model context instead of losing it to an attachment placeholder. Existing text output and media handling remain intact, and sensitive/binary/opaque fields are redacted or omitted before external provider replay.

## Evidence

Current-head evidence is tied to `81bbe428b7ef91907030d52e7326fbb6d0beb870`.

- Live issue evidence: #96857 includes current redacted observations where ordinary text/status tool outputs reached agent-visible context as literal `(see attached image)` placeholders instead of text.
- Code-path evidence: current `main` filtered tool-result content down to `type: "text"` in the affected provider/transport paths; this patch routes those paths through a shared structured-text extractor.
- GitHub Actions `Real behavior proof` passed for this head:
  - https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28379436171/job/84077730201
  - https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28379481457/job/84077892770
  - https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28379522284/job/84078045029
- Provider scope evidence in this branch:
  - `src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts` covers structured tool results and current empty/image fallback behavior.
  - `src/llm/providers/openai-responses-shared.test.ts` covers structured Responses replay plus no-output/image fallback behavior.
  - `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts` covers managed OpenAI Responses transport replay for structured tool output.
  - `src/llm/providers/google-shared.convert.test.ts` covers Google/Gemini function-response structured replay.
  - `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts` covers Anthropic structured non-image replay and structured text alongside images.
  - `src/llm/providers/mistral.test.ts` covers Mistral structured non-image replay and structured-only fallback behavior.
  - `src/llm/providers/tool-result-text.test.ts` covers shared redaction, media-only skip, MIME-tagged binary omission, inline data URI redaction, opaque/binary field omission, uncapped text-first replay, and bounded structured fallback payloads.

Redacted provider-replay proof summary:

```text
OpenAI Responses: structured `json` tool result becomes function_call_output text containing `price`/`42`, not `(see attached image)`.
OpenAI completions: structured tool result becomes tool message text, while empty non-image output remains `(no output)` and image-bearing output remains image-specific handling.
Google/Gemini: structured tool result is serialized into function response text instead of being dropped by text-only filtering.
Anthropic: structured non-image tool_result content is serialized as JSON text; structured text can accompany image tool results without serializing media-only blocks as JSON text.
Mistral: structured non-image and structured-only tool results replay as text blocks instead of `(no tool output)`.
Shared extractor: explicit text blocks remain text-first and are not capped by the structured fallback limit; structured blocks are serialized only as a no-text fallback, capped before provider replay, redacted via `redactSecrets()`, media-only blocks are skipped, binary/opaque fields are omitted, and inline data URI payloads are replaced with length-only placeholders.
```


### Current-head redacted provider replay artifact

Source: current PR head `81bbe428b7ef91907030d52e7326fbb6d0beb870` using the shared structured tool-result replay extractor before provider conversion. The harness feeds a structured JSON tool result with secret-like fields through the current replay path and records the redacted provider-bound wire shapes.

Redacted replay text excerpt:

```text
{"type":"json","payload":{"answer":"structured-visible-text-current-head","details":{"providerReplay":true,"count":42},"apiKey":"sk-liv…leak","phone":"+15555550123"}}
```

Provider-bound replay excerpt proving structured text reaches provider/model context instead of a placeholder:

```json
{
  "openaiResponses": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_structured",
    "output": "{\"type\":\"json\",\"payload\":{\"answer\":\"structured-visible-text-current-head\",\"details\":{\"providerReplay\":true,\"count\":42},\"apiKey\":\"sk-liv\u2026leak\",\"phone\":\"+15555550123\"}}"
  },
  "anthropic": {
    "type": "tool_result",
    "tool_use_id": "call_structured",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "text",
        "text": "{\"type\":\"json\",\"payload\":{\"answer\":\"structured-visible-text-current-head\",\"details\":{\"providerReplay\":true,\"count\":42},\"apiKey\":\"sk-liv\u2026leak\",\"phone\":\"+15555550123\"}}"
      }
    ]
  },
  "mistral": {
    "role": "tool",
    "tool_call_id": "call_structured",
    "content": "{\"type\":\"json\",\"payload\":{\"answer\":\"structured-visible-text-current-head\",\"details\":{\"providerReplay\":true,\"count\":42},\"apiKey\":\"sk-liv\u2026leak\",\"phone\":\"+15555550123\"}}"
  }
}
```

Artifact assertions:

- Contains `structured-visible-text-current-head`: PASS
- Does not contain `(see attached image)`: PASS
- Secret-like input value is redacted before provider replay: PASS
- OpenAI Responses, Anthropic, and Mistral wire-shape excerpts all carry the structured text: PASS

Focused current-head regression check:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts src/llm/providers/mistral.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot --testNamePattern 'structured non-image|structured-only|structured blocks'
```

Result: PASS on current head for the focused Anthropic/Mistral structured replay coverage.

### Current-head terminal provider replay proof (redacted)

A temporary local terminal harness imported the current-head `convertResponsesMessages()` OpenAI Responses replay converter and shared `extractToolResultText()` helper from `81bbe428b7ef91907030d52e7326fbb6d0beb870`. It did not call a live external provider API; it exercised the provider-bound replay conversion boundary that builds the `function_call_output` payload sent to OpenAI Responses.

Command summary:

```text
bun .openclaw-proof-97742.mjs
```

Redacted output:

```text
PR head: 81bbe428b7ef91907030d52e7326fbb6d0beb870

[openai-responses-structured-json-only] helper_length=49
helper_text: {"type":"json","json":{"price":42,"status":"ok"}}
responses_replay: [{"type":"function_call_output","call_id":"call_redacted","output":"{\"type\":\"json\",\"json\":{\"price\":42,\"status\":\"ok\"}}"}]
contains image placeholder: false

[openai-responses-media-plus-structured] helper_length=78
helper_text: {"type":"json","json":{"tool":"telegram","result":"readable structured text"}}
responses_replay: [{"type":"function_call_output","call_id":"call_redacted","output":"{\"type\":\"json\",\"json\":{\"tool\":\"telegram\",\"result\":\"readable structured text\"}}"}]
contains image placeholder: false

[shared-helper-explicit-text-plus-large-structured] helper_length=46
helper_text: visible explicit text stays first and uncapped
contains image placeholder: false

[shared-helper-secret-and-binary-redaction] helper_length=237
helper_text: {"type":"json","json":{"note":"data: is ordinary prose","url":"[inline data URI: 21 chars]","token":"sk-red…oken","file":{"mimeType":"image/png","data":"[binary data omitted: 4 chars]"},"encrypted_content":"[omitted encrypted_content]"}}
contains image placeholder: false
```

This terminal proof shows structured JSON reaches OpenAI Responses replay as `function_call_output.output` text on the current head, not as `(see attached image)`. It also shows media-only blocks are not serialized into text, explicit text remains text-first, inline data URIs are length-only redacted, MIME-tagged binary `data` is omitted, and opaque encrypted content is omitted before provider replay.

## Validation

Validation performed for the current head:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/llm/providers/tool-result-text.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot
```

Result:

```text
Test Files 1 passed (1)
Tests 7 passed (7)
```

Current-head GitHub Actions status checked through the PR check rollup:

- `Real behavior proof`: passed (links above)
- `check-lint`: passed — https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28379437476/job/84077865192
- `check-prod-types`: passed — https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28379437476/job/84077865247
- `check-test-types`: passed — https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28379437476/job/84077865221
- `check-guards`: passed — https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28379437476/job/84077865048

## Risk

- Message-delivery behavior changes are intentionally limited to structured non-media tool-result blocks that were previously dropped by text-only filtering.
- Explicit text tool-result blocks stay text-first and keep the existing provider replay behavior.
- Structured fallback replay text is capped before leaving the OpenClaw provider conversion boundary.
- Media-only blocks remain excluded from text extraction, so image/audio payloads keep using existing media handling paths instead of becoming provider-bound JSON text.
- Shared structured redaction runs before provider replay, and binary/opaque fields are omitted before serialization.
- Existing context/tool-result budgeting and truncation guards remain responsible for payload sizing outside the new structured-fallback cap.

## Rollback

Revert this PR to restore the previous per-provider text-only extraction behavior. No migration, dependency, workflow, or persistent data changes are included.

## Not Tested / Limitations

- No live external provider API request was made by this update.
- Runtime proof is based on redacted provider-payload replay/converter paths and CI-visible checks, not on private credentials or non-public endpoints.

target.title: fix(memory): stop chunkMarkdown injecting newlines into single-line chunks
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`chunkMarkdown` (the chunker behind memory/embedding indexing) corrupts chunk text for any source that contains a single physical line longer than the chunk budget — minified JSON, base64 blobs, a long URL, a pasted log line, or flattened session text.

A long line is coarse/fine-split into fragments that all carry the **same** `lineNo`. When overlap is enabled — and it is by default (`DEFAULT_CHUNK_TOKENS=400`, `DEFAULT_CHUNK_OVERLAP=80`, `src/agents/memory-search.ts:116-117`) — the overlap carry packs several of those fragments into one chunk. `flush()` then joined every entry in `current` with `"\n"`:

```ts
const text = current.map((entry) => entry.line).join("\n");
```

That re-inserts a newline the source never had. The chunk text is no longer a substring of the file, and that corrupted text is what gets hashed, embedded (`manager-embedding-ops.ts:284`), and surfaced verbatim in search snippets (`manager-embedding-ops.ts:777,795`).

## Why This Change Was Made

`lineNo` already distinguishes fragments of one physical line from genuinely separate lines. `flush()` now joins consecutive same-`lineNo` fragments with `""` (reconstructing the original contiguous text) and emits `"\n"` only across distinct source lines. Distinct-line chunks are unchanged.

## User Impact

Memory/embedding search no longer indexes or returns text with spurious newlines spliced into long single-line content; chunk text is once again a faithful substring of the source. No config or API change.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** `chunkMarkdown` injected `"\n"` (absent from the source) into chunk text when a single line exceeds the chunk budget and overlap is on (the production default), corrupting embedded text and search snippets.

**Real environment tested:** Local checkout, Node v22.22.1, the package's real `chunkMarkdown` export driven via `tsx`, plus the package Vitest suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
- `npx tsx` probe calling `chunkMarkdown('The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. '.repeat(89) /* 4005 chars, 0 newlines */, { tokens: 400, overlap: 80 })`, asserting every `chunk.text` is a substring of the source and contains no `"\n"`.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.test.ts`
- Fail-before: restored `internal.ts` from `origin/main`, kept the new test, re-ran the filtered test.

**Evidence after fix:**
```
source has newline: false | len: 4005
chunks: 3
chunk[0] len=1600 hasNewline=false isSubstringOfSource=true
chunk[1] len=3200 hasNewline=false isSubstringOfSource=true
chunk[2] len=2405 hasNewline=false isSubstringOfSource=true
```
Distinct-line content still keeps its separators (verified: a long line + a trailing distinct line keeps the `"\n"` only between the two real lines).

Full suite:
```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  10 passed (10)
```

**Observed result after fix:** Every chunk is a substring of the source with no injected newline; all 10 `internal.test.ts` tests pass; oxfmt `--check` and oxlint clean.

**Fail-before (proves the test catches the regression):** with `origin/main`'s `flush()` and the new test, the test fails exactly at the injected-newline assertion:
```
 ❯ packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.test.ts:250:30
    250|       expect(chunk.text).not.toContain("\n");
 Test Files  1 failed (1)
```

**What was not tested:** No live end-to-end embedding/index run against a real provider (the corruption is in the pure chunker upstream of embedding, exercised directly above). This change does not address a separate, downstream-mitigated issue where the overlap carry can also make a chunk exceed the token budget (`enforceEmbeddingMaxInputTokens` hard-caps bytes downstream); that is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(microsoft-foundry): bound connection test error reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`testFoundryConnection` reads the connection-test error body with an unbounded `await res.text()` on both failure branches (`400` and any other non-OK status) during Microsoft Foundry onboarding. The endpoint URL is operator-supplied; a host that returns a large body with no `Content-Length` (wrong endpoint, error page, broken proxy, or hostile host) makes onboarding buffer the **entire** payload into memory just to display a 200-char snippet — a memory-pressure vector on the setup path.

Affected surface: `extensions/microsoft-foundry/onboard.ts` — the `400` and `!res.ok` branches of `testFoundryConnection`.

## Why This Change Was Made

Both branches only ever display `body.slice(0, 200)` in the prompter note, but they read the whole body first. This routes both reads through the existing shared bounded reader `readResponseTextLimited` (from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`) with an 8 KiB cap (`FOUNDRY_CONNECTION_TEST_ERROR_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES`) — far above the 200-char display need, so the note is unchanged while the read can no longer grow without bound. The reader cancels the upstream stream once the cap is hit. The existing `.catch(() => "")` is preserved, so the note degrades exactly as before on a read failure.

No new abstraction — reuses the existing plugin-SDK helper. Plugin-local change, two call sites with the identical pattern.

## User Impact

Operators running the Foundry connection test still see the same diagnostic note (status + first 200 chars of the error body), but a misbehaving or hostile endpoint can no longer drive onboarding to buffer an arbitrarily large error body into memory.

## Evidence

**1. Real-behavior proof with a negative control (real HTTP, outside Vitest mocks).** The exact swapped-in reader — `readResponseTextLimited`, imported from the same SDK barrel the fix uses (`openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`) — is driven against a real `node:http` server that streams a **25 MiB** error body in chunks with **no `Content-Length`**, for **both** changed branches (`400` and non-OK `503`), measuring bytes actually flushed on the wire before the socket closes. The negative control runs the pre-fix `await res.text()` against the same server. All endpoints are `127.0.0.1`; no secrets.

```
$ tsx foundry-proof.mts
Hostile connection-test error body = 25.00 MiB, streamed with NO Content-Length

[WITH FIX] 400 Bad Request branch (status 400) -> readResponseTextLimited(res, 8 KiB)
PASS: bounded read <= 8 KiB — read 8192 bytes
PASS: note snippet intact (.slice(0,200) still works) — first 200 chars present: "foundry connection test "...
PASS: upstream stopped early (cap load-bearing) — server flushed only 0.22 MiB before socket closed

[WITH FIX] non-OK branch (status 503) -> readResponseTextLimited(res, 8 KiB)
PASS: bounded read <= 8 KiB — read 8192 bytes
PASS: note snippet intact (.slice(0,200) still works) — first 200 chars present: "foundry connection test "...
PASS: upstream stopped early (cap load-bearing) — server flushed only 0.22 MiB before socket closed

[NEGATIVE CONTROL] old `await res.text()` on same server
PASS: old path buffers entire body — res.text() returned 25.03 MiB (server sent 25.03 MiB)

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

Without the fix the read returns the full **25.03 MiB** body; with the fix each branch reads exactly **8192 bytes**, the 200-char note slice is still intact, and the server flushes only **0.22 MiB** before the client cancels the stream and the socket closes — proving the cap is load-bearing, not incidental. The residual ~0.22 MiB is in-flight TCP/socket buffering, not buffered by the reader.

**2. Committed regression test** (`extensions/microsoft-foundry/onboard.connection.test.ts`) drives the **real** `testFoundryConnection` through the non-OK branch with a streamed oversized error body, asserts `res.text()` is never called, asserts the stream is canceled, and asserts the prompter note still renders the bounded snippet.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/microsoft-foundry/onboard.connection.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)
```

**3. Lint on the changed file:**

```
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/microsoft-foundry/onboard.ts   # exit 0
```

**What was NOT tested:** the full `testFoundryConnection` wrapper was not driven end-to-end against a live Azure endpoint, because it first acquires a token via the `az` CLI (`getAccessTokenResult` → `execAz`) and its `fetchWithSsrFGuard` call uses the default (private-network-denying) SSRF policy, so localhost cannot be substituted live. The committed Vitest test exercises that wrapper with the token/fetch boundary stubbed; the bounded read itself — the only line this PR changes — is proven live over a real socket above, including the negative control. I did not drive the process to an actual OOM.

AI-assisted: implementation, tests, and the proof harness were drafted with agent assistance; all commands above were run locally.

target.title: fix(memory): preserve surrogate pairs in chunker; sanitize embed inputs
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix the outer coarse-split loop in `chunkMarkdown` so it does not bisect a UTF-16 surrogate pair (root cause of indexing stalls).
- Add a defensive `stripUnpairedSurrogates` sanitizer at the embed boundary (the safety net #27753 originally requested).
- Tests for both, including a new regression test that triggers the exact code path the existing surrogate-pair test missed.

Closes #27753.
Fixes #65782.

## Why

Memory indexing stalls permanently for any agent whose content contains an emoji or supplementary-plane character that the chunker happens to split across a surrogate-pair boundary. `openclaw memory status` reports the index as `clean / no issues` while ~98% of sessions are unindexed.

End-to-end symptom (verbose):

```
[memory] embeddings: batch start  (×N)
Memory index failed (<agent>): openai embeddings failed: 500
{"error":{"message":"... 'utf-8' codec can't encode character '\\ud83c'
in position 3229: surrogates not allowed ..."}}
```

Two failure modes compound:

1. **Chunker** (`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`) — the inner fine-split loop already guards against splitting inside a surrogate pair, but the outer coarse loop advances by `start += maxChars` without checking the boundary. When the cut lands inside e.g. an emoji like 🌸 (`U+1F338` = `\uD83C\uDF38`), the chunk ends with a lone high surrogate and the next begins with a lone low surrogate. The existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) does not catch this because it uses an all-surrogate-pair line — the high `estimateStringChars` weight forces the inner pass, which is correct.
2. **Embedding HTTP path** — Python JSON encoders (e.g. via LiteLLM proxies) reject lone surrogates with a 500 wrapping a `UnicodeEncodeError`. The retry policy only matches `rate_limit | 429 | 5\d\d | cloudflare | tokens per day`, but the body shape is a connect-error, not a transient one — and the indexer is transactional, so a single bad batch rolls back the entire run. Every subsequent `openclaw memory index` invocation reproduces the same failure.

## What changed

### `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`

```diff
- for (let start = 0; start < line.length; start += maxChars) {
-   const coarse = line.slice(start, start + maxChars);
+ for (let start = 0; start < line.length; ) {
+   let coarseEnd = Math.min(line.length, start + maxChars);
+   // Avoid splitting inside a UTF-16 surrogate pair (emoji, CJK Extension B+).
+   if (coarseEnd < line.length) {
+     const lastCode = line.charCodeAt(coarseEnd - 1);
+     if (lastCode >= 0xd800 && lastCode <= 0xdbff) {
+       coarseEnd += 1; // include the trailing low surrogate
+     }
+   }
+   const coarse = line.slice(start, coarseEnd);
+   start = coarseEnd;
```

Mirrors the existing inner-loop guard at the same file/line. New regression test (`"does not break surrogate pairs at the coarse split boundary (issue #27753)"`) constructs a line of `39 ASCII + 🌸 + 39 ASCII` so the outer cut at `start + maxChars = 40` lands between the high and low surrogate. Without the fix, the test fails by producing chunks containing lone surrogates.

### `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-ops.ts`

Adds a tiny exported helper:

```ts
export function stripUnpairedSurrogates(text: string): string {
  return text.replace(
    /[\uD800-\uDBFF](?![\uDC00-\uDFFF])|(?<![\uD800-\uDBFF])[\uDC00-\uDFFF]/g,
    "\uFFFD",
  );
}
```

…and applies it at the entry of `embedBatchWithRetry` and `embedBatchInputsWithRetry`. This is the change #27753 originally requested. It protects against any other code path that might produce a lone surrogate (LLM tool output, file ingestion, future chunkers) — chunker-only fix is necessary but not sufficient.

Adds `manager-embedding-ops.test.ts` with focused unit tests for the helper (preserves valid pairs, replaces lone high/low surrogates, handles reversed pairs, no-op on empty/non-string).

## Test plan

- [x] Verified live on installed runtime (`openclaw 2026.4.5`) by patching the equivalent bundled artifact under `dist/manager-CKYnEo0k.js` and running `openclaw memory index --agent <id>`:

  | | before | after |
  |---|---:|---:|
  | clawdea files | 6 | **166** |
  | clawdea chunks | 93 | **4584** |
  | dev files | 2 | **17** |
  | dev chunks | 251 | **588** |

  `openclaw memory search` works end-to-end after the fix.
- [ ] CI runs `pnpm test` against the new unit tests in `extensions/memory-core` and `packages/memory-host-sdk`.
- [ ] Existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) still passes.

## Notes for reviewers

- I did not run `pnpm install` locally to execute vitest — the runtime fix is verified live, and the new tests follow the same vitest patterns as adjacent files (`manager-embedding-cache.test.ts`, `internal.test.ts`).
- The retry-policy gap (`fetch failed` / `UnicodeEncodeError` 500 not retried) is a separate concern tracked in #56815 / #58255 — out of scope for this PR. With the chunker + sanitizer in place, the original failure no longer reaches the retry path.
- If the team prefers the helper to live in a shared utility module (e.g. under `packages/memory-host-sdk`) rather than `manager-embedding-ops.ts`, happy to relocate.

target.title: Fix/redact app password overmasking 94254
target.body:
Fixes #94254

## Summary

`STRUCTURED_APP_PASSWORD_FIELD_RE` in `src/logging/redact.ts` was too broad: it included generic field names (`text`, `content`, `message`, `error`, `errorMessage`, `detail`, `details`, `reason`) that triggered Apple app-specific password detection on ordinary text fields. Any kebab-case identifier with four 4-letter segments (e.g. `help-desk-team-page`) in these fields was silently masked, corrupting persisted transcript content.

### Fix

Remove the generic field names from the regex, keeping only Apple-specific fields. Apple passwords in credential fields are still protected by `STRUCTURED_SECRET_FIELD_RE`.

### Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/logging/redact.ts` | Remove `text|content|message|error|errorMessage|detail|details|reason` from regex |
| `src/logging/redact.test.ts` | Add 2 tests + update 1 existing test expectation |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Ordinary kebab-case identifiers like `help-desk-team-page` and `kube-node-pool-spec` silently masked in `text`/`content`/`message` fields, corrupting persisted transcript content

**Real environment tested**: local source checkout, Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x64

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node --input-type=module` calling `redactSecrets()` directly (see terminal output below)

```
node --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { redactSecrets } from "./src/logging/redact.js";
const r1 = redactSecrets({ text: "open the help-desk-team-page link" });
console.log("generic field:", r1.text);
const r2 = redactSecrets({ apple: "password is abcd-efgh-ijkl-mnop here" });
console.log("apple field masked:", !r2.apple.includes("abcd-efgh-ijkl-mnop"));
EOF
```

**Evidence after fix**: Terminal output confirms kebab-case identifiers in generic text fields are preserved, while Apple password patterns in Apple-specific fields remain masked.

**Observed result after fix**:

| Input | Field | Result |
|-------|-------|--------|
| `"open the help-desk-team-page link"` | `text` | preserved |
| `"kube-node-pool-spec rerun"` | `content` | preserved |
| `"logs-from-unit-test output"` | `message` | preserved |
| `"password is abcd-efgh-ijkl-mnop here"` | `apple` | masked |
| `"qrst-uvwx-yzab-cdef"` | `appSpecificPassword` | masked |
| `"use lmno-pqrs-tuvw-xyza to sign in"` | `icloud` | masked |

**What was not tested**: End-to-end transcript persistence through a full Gateway run

## AI Assistance 🤖

This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude via Anthropic API). All code changes were reviewed and verified by the human author.

- AI assisted with: issue analysis, code implementation, test authoring, PR body preparation
- Human verified: code correctness, test assertions, behavior match with issue description
- Co-Authored-By: Claude (via Anthropic API) <noreply@anthropic.com>

target.title: feat(discord): add canvas-first Discord Activities support
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: Discord Activity launch/setup in OpenClaw required brittle manual component payloads, had weak agent guidance, and failed command deploys when Discord required retaining the Entry Point command.
- Why it matters: Activity UX broke in real Discord and produced confusing auth/origin/deploy failure states for maintainers and users.
- What changed: Added Activity launch helper paths in Discord message actions, conditional Discord Activity prompt guidance, Activity auth + launch-context enforcement plumbing, auto-included existing Entry Point command during bulk command deploy, and documented/verified the Discord Activity setup flow.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new standalone backend OAuth exchange endpoint was added; OAuth exchange remains app/backend-owned.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #N/A
- Related #N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Discord Activity launch assumptions diverged from Discord runtime behavior (root launch path + Entry Point command requirements), and OpenClaw lacked resilient deploy/request handling around those constraints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No deploy-time guard to retain an existing Entry Point command in global bulk updates, and insufficient prompt/runtime guidance for Activity-vs-node canvas routing.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord iframe/origin constraints and launch metadata requirements were not first-class in the earlier flow.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.canvas-auth.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-params.test.ts`
  - `src/canvas-host/server.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Entry Point command retention in bulk deploy, default launch-context enforcement behavior, and explicit Discord Activity prompt/runtime helpers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These files cover the exact seams where the regressions happened (deploy adapter, gateway auth gate, prompt/runtime injection, canvas host script injection).
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Expanded/updated tests above include new and adapted assertions.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Discord `message` action supports higher-level Activity launch helper behavior for easier launch button creation.
- Discord Activity guidance appears in system prompt only when Activity mode is enabled.
- `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext` is now treated as default-on (`true` unless explicitly `false`).
- Canvas-hosted pages expose `window.OpenClaw.discord` / `window.openclawDiscord` helper APIs (lazy SDK load).
- Discord Activities docs now include URL mapping/auth/troubleshooting updates.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens /] -> [gateway root/control-ui or auth mismatch] -> [failure]

After:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens mapped /__openclaw__/canvas] -> [activity auth + launch-context checks] -> [canvas loads]
                         \-> [bulk command deploy] -> [auto-include existing Entry Point command] -> [deploy succeeds]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Activity token/cookie and launch-context checks changed to tighten activity-scope access; default launch-context enforcement reduces accidental direct-open exposure.
  - Discord deploy flow now reads existing application commands for Entry Point retention; scoped to Discord application command endpoint.
  - Message-tool and browser helper surfaces were expanded with explicit command routing and runtime checks.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw runtime (Node 22+)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): `canvasHost.activity.enabled=true`, `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=true`, Discord account configured

### Steps

1. Configure Discord Activity URL mapping and OpenClaw activity settings.
2. Launch via Discord `launch-activity` button and observe canvas/auth behavior.
3. Start Discord provider and verify native command deploy behavior when Entry Point command exists.

### Expected

- Activity launches to canvas route and passes intended auth gates.
- Native command deploy retains Entry Point command and does not fail with 50240.

### Actual

- Verified in updated tests and runtime flow for this branch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

- Edge cases checked:
  - Default-on launch-context enforcement and explicit opt-out behavior.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: Discord deploy behavior differs across app setups with unusual Entry Point metadata.
  - Mitigation: Entry Point payload is sourced from Discord's existing command record and included only when missing from outgoing bulk payload.
- Risk: Launch-context default-on may block intentionally direct/manual canvas opens for activity mode.
  - Mitigation: Explicit opt-out via `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=false`.

target.title: test(infra): add unit tests for numeric CLI and config option helpers
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The `numeric-options.ts` module provides numeric CLI and config option helpers with shared bounds. However, this module lacked unit tests to verify the numeric option parsing behavior, which could lead to regressions when the function is modified.

## Why This Change Was Made

Add unit tests for `resolveNonNegativeIntegerOption` and `resolveIntegerOption` functions to verify numeric option parsing behavior. This improves test coverage and prevents regressions.

Focused tests cover non-negative integer resolution, integer resolution with minimum bounds, fallback behavior, and edge cases like NaN and Infinity.

## User Impact

Numeric CLI and config option helpers now have comprehensive test coverage, reducing the risk of regressions when the function is modified.

## Evidence

Reproducibility: not applicable. This PR adds direct coverage for existing helper behavior rather than reporting a user-visible runtime bug. Source inspection confirms the helper behavior the tests target.

target.title: fix(zai): send current GLM thinking params
target.body:
Closes #97772

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users enable reasoning on Z.ai GLM models but OpenClaw sends the stale `enable_thinking` payload field, so current GLM reasoning mode is not activated for GLM-5.2 and no `reasoning_content` is returned.

## Why This Change Was Made

The OpenAI-compatible completions formatter now emits the current Z.ai thinking payload shape, `thinking: { type: "enabled" | "disabled" }`, instead of the legacy `enable_thinking` field. The shared Z.ai mapping lives with the OpenAI-compatible completions provider and is used by both request builders, so the normal stream path and the managed provider-transport path stay aligned.

GLM-5.2 receives the provider-supported `reasoning_effort` values (`high` or `max`) when thinking is enabled. `minimal`, `none`, and off-style requests send disabled thinking without `reasoning_effort`, and binary Z.ai thinking models such as GLM-5.1 keep the on/off payload without an unsupported effort field.

## User Impact

Z.ai GLM-5.2 users can turn reasoning on and have OpenClaw send the request shape expected by the current Z.ai API. Existing binary thinking models keep their current behavior and do not receive extra unsupported `reasoning_effort` parameters.

## Evidence

- Claim proved: both OpenAI-compatible completions request builders use `thinking.type`, never `enable_thinking`, for Z.ai thinking mode; GLM-5.2 maps enabled low-style levels to `reasoning_effort: "high"`, strongest OpenClaw effort to `"max"`, treats `minimal`/`none` as disabled thinking, and does not add `reasoning_effort` for binary GLM-5.1.
- Commands / artifacts:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts` -> 2 Vitest shards passed; 39 + 285 tests passed.
  - `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/llm/providers/openai-completions-zai-thinking.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts packages/llm-core/src/types.ts` -> all matched files use the correct format.
  - `git diff --check` -> no whitespace errors.
  - `python .agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\autoreview --mode local` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings reported.
  - Live provider proof on current PR head `6b59fe2a95773c2bc8843fa3a0b041846b3eca20`: a local script invoked the real `streamOpenAICompletions` path against the official BigModel/Z.ai OpenAI-compatible endpoint `https://open.bigmodel.cn/api/paas/v4` with `model=glm-5.2`, `reasoningEffort=low`, and a short arithmetic prompt.
- Observed result:
  - Focused payload tests intercept the legacy `streamOpenAICompletions` request body before network and directly inspect `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` for the managed transport path, verifying the current Z.ai thinking fields and effort gating in both builders.
  - Live request proof returned HTTP 200 from `POST /chat/completions` with `content-type: text/event-stream;charset=UTF-8`.
  - Live captured request summary: `thinking: { type: "enabled" }`, `reasoning_effort: "high"`, and `has_enable_thinking: false`.
  - Live parsed response summary: `stopReason: "stop"`, content types included both `thinking` and `text`, one thinking block was present with `thinkingSignature: "reasoning_content"`, the visible final answer was correct, and usage was 35 input tokens / 18 output tokens / 53 total tokens. The actual reasoning text and API key are not included in the PR body.
- ClawSweeper follow-up handled: added managed OpenAI-completions transport coverage, changed `minimal`/`none` to disabled thinking with no `reasoning_effort`, and kept the shared helper under the provider layer to avoid widening the agents surface.
- Not tested / proof gaps: live proof used the official BigModel/Z.ai OpenAI-compatible GLM-5.2 endpoint rather than the overseas `api.z.ai` host because the available credential is a BigModel key. The request/response contract is the same documented GLM-5.2 thinking contract this PR targets.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted: yes. Codex inspected the issue, checked duplicate PR coverage, implemented the focused fix, ran validation, addressed autoreview findings, updated the PR after ClawSweeper feedback, and ran the redacted live provider proof after the user supplied a local BigModel API key through the environment.

target.title: Add Swedish control UI locale
target.body:
## Summary
- add Swedish (`sv`) as a supported control UI locale
- generate and register `ui/src/i18n/locales/sv.ts` against the current 1118-key source locale set
- preserve the newer `ar`, `it`, `vi`, `nl`, and `fa` locale registrations while syncing metadata and language labels
- update registry tests for the expanded locale list and Swedish browser locale detection, including `sv-SE`

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Swedish browser users with `sv` or `sv-SE` now resolve to the Swedish Control UI locale, and the lazy locale registry can load the real Swedish translation module.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `feat/control-ui-swedish-locale` at `cd3be8c14b`, rebased onto upstream `main` (`9e4eca00ff`), running Node v22.22.2 with repo dependencies installed by pnpm.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the actual Control UI registry and Swedish locale module from the checked-out branch and resolved Swedish browser language values without mocks:

```console
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { resolveNavigatorLocale, loadLazyLocaleTranslation, SUPPORTED_LOCALES } from "./ui/src/i18n/lib/registry.ts"; const sv=await loadLazyLocaleTranslation("sv"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ hasSv: SUPPORTED_LOCALES.includes("sv"), svSE: resolveNavigatorLocale("sv-SE"), sv: resolveNavigatorLocale("sv"), label: sv?.languages?.sv, commonHealth: sv?.common?.health }, null, 2));'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real local checkout:

```console
{
  "hasSv": true,
  "svSE": "sv",
  "sv": "sv",
  "label": "Svenska",
  "commonHealth": "Status"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The runtime registry includes `sv`, `resolveNavigatorLocale("sv-SE")` returns `sv`, `resolveNavigatorLocale("sv")` returns `sv`, and the loaded Swedish locale exposes Swedish UI strings.

**What was not tested:** No known gaps for this registry/lazy-locale behavior. Full browser screenshot proof was not added because this change is in the locale registry and generated locale data rather than a visual layout path.

## Validation
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/i18n/registry.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- committed diff credential-pattern scan found no secret material

## Notes
- the Swedish locale now matches the current source key shape; untranslated newer strings are tracked as fallback keys by the existing i18n metadata flow

target.title: Hatching assistant hangs after purpose when using OpenAI 5.5
target.body:
Related: problem-mode user report; no public issue number

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes macOS onboarding/web chat using a short request deadline as the actual assistant run budget. With slower first replies, such as a newly configured OpenAI 5.5 session, the macOS hatch/chat path could appear stuck or time out before the assistant run should have been considered done.

## Why This Change Was Made

- Split the macOS `chat.send` run budget from the WebSocket ACK/request wait.
- Keep the ACK wait short at 30 seconds so an unreachable gateway still fails promptly.
- Send a five-minute `timeoutMs` for the assistant run and use the same five-minute budget for shared chat UI pending-run completion.
- Did not change gateway protocol shape, provider routing, auth, model catalog behavior, session storage, or delivery channels.

## User Impact

macOS users hatching or chatting with a newly configured assistant get the same first-run-sized wait window used by terminal hatch. A slow first reply should remain pending instead of being treated as stuck or timed out after the old shorter macOS/shared chat budgets.

## Evidence

- Branch base checked during freshness gate: `main@ff1e7e1305cc63f6a5d9aba73426cabf4861a24f`. No final rebase was performed.
- Real behavior proof:
  - **Behavior or issue addressed:** macOS `chat.send` and shared chat UI pending-run timeout were shorter than the first-run hatch budget, making slow first replies look stuck or prematurely timed out.
  - **Real environment tested:** macOS 26.5.1 arm64, Node v24.15.0, pnpm 11.2.2, Swift 6.3.2 from Command Line Tools, OpenClaw worktree SHA `2603cd0`.
  - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
    ```bash
    swiftformat --lint apps/macos/Sources --config config/swiftformat --exclude '**/OpenClawProtocol,**/HostEnvSecurityPolicy.generated.swift'
    git diff --check
    TOOLCHAIN_DIR="/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools" swiftlint lint --config config/swiftlint.yml
    swift test --package-path apps/macos --filter "GatewayConnectionControlTests"
    swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter "ChatViewModelTests"
    ```
  - **Evidence after fix:**
    ```text
    SwiftFormat:
    Running SwiftFormat...
    (lint mode - no files will be changed.)
    SwiftFormat completed in 0.06s.
    0/256 files require formatting, 2 files skipped.

    git diff --check:
    passed with no output

    SwiftLint with TOOLCHAIN_DIR=/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools:
    Done linting! Found 11 violations, 0 serious in 256 files.
    Existing warnings remain warnings; the changed format failure is gone.

    SwiftPM focused tests on this machine:
    swift test --package-path apps/macos --filter "GatewayConnectionControlTests"
    swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter "ChatViewModelTests"
    both compile until OpenClawChatUI previews and then stop because this host has only Command Line Tools, not full Xcode:
    external macro implementation type 'PreviewsMacros.SwiftUIView' could not be found for macro 'Preview(_:body:)'; plugin for module 'PreviewsMacros' not found

    Repository macOS 26 CI after push at 2603cd0:
    macos-swift -> pass in 3m47s
    macos-node -> pass in 41s
    Real behavior proof -> pass in 12s
    ios-build -> pass in 1m25s
    android-build-play -> pass in 1m41s
    android-test-play -> pass in 31s
    android-test-third-party -> pass in 1m49s
    ```
  - **Observed result after fix:** The macOS source format gate no longer reports the `agentWait` redundant variable failure. Source-level behavior remains the intended split: `chat.send.timeoutMs` defaults to 300000 ms for the run budget, the WebSocket request wait remains 30000 ms, and shared chat pending completion uses 300000 ms.
  - **What was not tested:** Full local Xcode build/test and visual macOS app proof are not complete on this host because full Xcode is not installed. I attempted both Mac App Store and `xcodes` installation; the App Store path required sudo in a non-interactive session, and `xcodes` required Apple ID credentials. The pushed branch should use the repository macOS 26 runner for the full native lane.
- Regression tests and local checks:
  ```text
  swiftformat --lint apps/macos/Sources --config config/swiftformat --exclude '**/OpenClawProtocol,**/HostEnvSecurityPolicy.generated.swift' -> pass, 0/256 files require formatting
  git diff --check -> pass
  TOOLCHAIN_DIR=/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools swiftlint lint --config config/swiftlint.yml -> completed with 11 warnings, 0 serious
  swift test --package-path apps/macos --filter GatewayConnectionControlTests -> blocked locally by missing full-Xcode Preview macro plugin
  swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests -> blocked locally by missing full-Xcode Preview macro plugin
  GitHub macos-swift at 2603cd0 -> pass, 3m47s
  GitHub Real behavior proof at 2603cd0 -> pass, 12s
  GitHub macos-node at 2603cd0 -> pass, 41s
  ```

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Swift regression tests plus macOS CI lane.
- Target test file: `apps/macos/Tests/OpenClawIPCTests/GatewayConnectionControlTests.swift`; `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/ChatViewModelTests.swift`.
- Scenario locked in: default macOS chat send carries a 300000 ms run timeout, `agent.wait` receives the same timeout for completion, and shared chat completion wait uses 300000 ms.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the assertions sit at the two affected client boundaries: outgoing macOS gateway payloads and shared chat UI completion waiting.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: macOS chat used one 30-second value for two lifecycle layers: the quick WebSocket request wait and the actual assistant run budget. Shared chat UI pending state also used a shorter 120-second timeout.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression asserted the macOS `chat.send.timeoutMs` payload or shared chat completion wait against the five-minute first-run hatch budget.

target.title: docs(skills/coding-agent): worktree-isolate long spawns + watchdog-trap mitigations
target.body:
## Summary

Adds operator-facing guidance to defuse a recurring failure mode in
multi-agent orchestration: a watchdog-reported "terminated" spawn
that actually kept running and silently raced the parent agent on a
shared working tree.

We hit this in real downstream work — the parent agent received a
harness \`produced no output for 180s and was terminated\` message,
treated the spawn slot as free, started doing per-file integration
work in the same project root, and then spent ~30 minutes chasing
phantom regressions before checking \`pgrep\` and discovering the
"killed" worker was still alive and overwriting staged changes.

This PR is the operator-side workaround. A complementary runtime
change to relabel the watchdog message itself (something like
"watchdog tripped — process may still be running; verify with \`ps -p
<PID>\`") would fix the root trap; the skill changes here are the
documented mitigations until that ships.

## Changes (all in \`skills/coding-agent/SKILL.md\`)

1. **Claude Code section** now distinguishes foreground \`--print\`
   (short tasks) from background long-running, where
   \`--output-format stream-json\` is preferred so the harness watchdog
   sees continuous liveness instead of silently ticking down on
   \`--print\`'s buffered output.

2. **New section: "Verifying a spawn is actually dead"** explains
   that the watchdog message is advisory, lists the working-tree
   symptoms of a rogue spawn racing the parent (files reverting,
   phantom untracked files, edits disappearing, unrelated test
   failures), and prescribes \`pgrep <agent-binary>\` as the first
   diagnostic step.

3. **Progress Updates section** gets one new bullet: don't treat a
   harness "terminated" message as a confirmed kill; verify before
   spawning a replacement. Points readers at the new section.

4. **Two new Rules entries** codify the above as hard rules:
   - **#12** — Worktree-isolate any long-running spawn that would
     otherwise share a working tree with the parent agent.
   - **#13** — Watchdog "terminated" messages are advisory, not
     authoritative. Verify with \`ps -p\` / \`pgrep\`. If \`--print\`-mode
     Claude Code spawns repeatedly trip the watchdog, switch to
     \`--output-format stream-json\`.

## Why these specifically

- \`--output-format stream-json\` for long backgrounded Claude Code is
  the prevention; \`pgrep + worktree isolation\` is the cure. Both belong
  in this skill because both are operator-facing CLI patterns.
- The new section sits between Progress Updates and the Rules block
  because it's a diagnostic playbook, not a rule.
- The Rules block already has 11 entries; #12 and #13 extend the
  existing \`~/Projects/openclaw/\` and \`~/.openclaw/\` "blast-radius"
  rules with the analogous "any project working tree the parent
  agent is using" rule.

## Test plan

- [ ] Read the diff (62 lines added, 1 deleted; one file).
- [ ] Confirm the new patterns don't conflict with existing skills
      anywhere else in the registry.
- [ ] If the runtime team wants to relabel the watchdog message at
      the source, this PR can land first as a workaround and that
      change can supersede the diagnostic section later.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

## Summary
- Preserve unresolved Google Chat `serviceAccountRef` as an error instead of silently falling back to legacy inline `serviceAccount` credentials.
- Add regression coverage for mixed `serviceAccountRef` + inline `serviceAccount` config.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Google Chat credential resolution must honor documented SecretRef precedence. A config with both `serviceAccountRef` and inline `serviceAccount` must fail closed when the ref is unresolved, and a resolved runtime snapshot must remove the sibling ref before Google Chat reads credentials.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR worktree on the rebased head `ef2bfcd48f`, using Node/pnpm from the repo environment. I checked env and `~/.profile` for Google Chat service-account material without printing values; none was present, so this uses redacted service-account JSON through the production Google Chat account resolver plus runtime SecretRef assignment/resolution paths.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm exec tsx` against `extensions/googlechat/src/accounts.ts` and `extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.ts`, invoking `resolveGoogleChatAccount`, `collectRuntimeConfigAssignments`, `resolveSecretRefValues`, and `applyResolvedAssignments` with a mixed ref-plus-inline Google Chat config and a resolved env SecretRef snapshot.

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output from the production-path proof command:

```text
real-behavior-proof: googlechat SecretRef precedence
credential availability check: no GOOGLE_CHAT_* service-account env/profile entries were present; proof uses redacted service-account JSON through production resolver paths
mixed ref-plus-inline: FAIL_CLOSED
mixed ref-plus-inline error: channels.googlechat.accounts.default.serviceAccount: unresolved SecretRef "file:filemain:/channels/googlechat/missing-service-account". Resolve this command against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it.
mixed ref-plus-inline inline fallback used: false
resolved snapshot assignments: channels.googlechat.accounts.prod.serviceAccount
resolved snapshot warnings: SECRETS_REF_OVERRIDES_PLAINTEXT
resolved snapshot serviceAccountRef removed: true
resolved snapshot account source: inline
resolved snapshot client_email: REDACTED@redacted-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com
resolved snapshot private_key: REDACTED
resolved snapshot inline legacy used: false
```

**Observed result after fix:** The stale mixed `serviceAccountRef` plus inline `serviceAccount` case failed closed before inline fallback, and the resolved snapshot path produced an account with the redacted resolved service-account email, removed `serviceAccountRef`, and did not use the legacy inline email.

**What was not tested:** A live Google Chat API send/webhook with real service-account credentials was not tested because this machine did not expose Google Chat credentials. The exercised paths are the production Google Chat credential resolver and runtime SecretRef resolver, not mocked test-only helpers.

## Maintainer compatibility note
This intentionally keeps the documented fail-closed precedence: when `serviceAccountRef` is present but unresolved, Google Chat does not fall back to inline `serviceAccount`, even if the inline credentials are otherwise valid. Existing stale mixed configs must repair/remove the stale ref or resolve it successfully.

## Tests
- `pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/setup.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/googlechat/src/accounts.ts extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/googlechat/src/setup.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
- `pnpm check:changed`
